answer: Argon2id is used with memory cost 64MB, iterations 3, and parallelism 4.
Refresh tokens expire in 7 days and rotate on every use.
The Notification Service runs on port 9200.
The database is RDS PostgreSQL 15.4, upgraded from 14.9.
API Gateway throttling is 5000 req/s burst and 2000 req/s steady state.
Argon2id was chosen over bcrypt for better resistance to side-channel attacks.
Drizzle was chosen over Prisma because it has no runtime dependencies and provides SQL-like TypeScript; migrations are co-located in /db/migrations.
Both Portfolio and Notification services verify RS256 JWTs using public keys from the Auth Service JWKS endpoint at /.well-known/jwks.json.
Rate limiting is enforced at ALB so abusive traffic is dropped before it reaches containers, reducing DDoS impact on application resources.

Request flow is: client -> API Gateway -> custom authorizer Lambda validates JWT via Auth JWKS and injects claims -> VPC Link/internal ALB/App Mesh path to Portfolio Service -> Portfolio checks role/claims (including hierarchy logic) before allowing rebalance.

If JWKS is down, token validation paths are affected (API Gateway custom authorizer, Portfolio Service, Notification Service, and other JWT verifiers). Existing cached keys can provide short continuity where cache exists; the stated JWKS key-cache TTL is 5 minutes. No longer grace period is specified.
Real-time portfolio alerting depends on Notification Service WebSocket support plus API Gateway WebSocket API; it is planned for Q3 and not yet active.
Yes. The UTC+13/+14 expiry bug was fixed by using Unix timestamps for all token expiry calculations and avoiding Date object-based logic.

Use the existing Notification Service webhook channel: enterprises register endpoints via POST /api/v1/notifications/webhooks/register, Auth emits auth-event notifications into that channel, and delivery uses the existing exponential-backoff retry policy (5 attempts over 24 hours).
With 20 DB connections per Auth container and 5 instances, maximum simultaneous Auth DB connections are 100.

The Auth connection pool setting (20/container) is separate from the Portfolio Celery memory leak. The Celery issue is worker-memory growth during large recalculations, not an Auth pool behavior, though both services still share the same PostgreSQL cluster.
No. Fargate Spot is not suitable for the Auth Service because Auth is always-on and requires zero-downtime behavior; Spot interruptions conflict with that requirement.
The stateless-auth/no-sessions architecture decision prohibits adding an in-memory session cache to Auth Service.

30-day backup retention provides many restore points and supports disaster recovery, while RPO 1 hour/RTO 4 hours define recovery objectives; cross-region replication further supports meeting those targets.
The connection string now requires ?sslmode=verify-full&sslrootcert=global-bundle.pem.
The tax_lots columns are: id, account_id, security_id, acquisition_date, cost_basis, quantity, adjusted, created_at.
MFA changes login so that when MFA is enabled, login requires a TOTP step; setup is via POST /api/v1/auth/mfa/setup which returns a QR code URI.
10 recovery codes are generated at MFA setup, each is single-use, and each is permanently consumed once used.
Webhook retries use exponential backoff with 5 attempts across 24 hours.

Credential/security posture includes: Argon2id password hashing (64MB/3/4), RS256 JWT signing with JWKS-based public key distribution, refresh-token rotation, PII encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM per-tenant keys, secrets in AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation, mTLS between services via App Mesh/Envoy, and RDS TLS verification via sslmode=verify-full with global-bundle.pem.

With MFA enabled: client sends login request -> API Gateway validates request schema and routes to Auth -> Auth verifies password against Argon2id hash in PostgreSQL -> Auth requires TOTP challenge -> on successful TOTP, Auth issues access token (15 min) and refresh token (7 days, rotating on use) -> responses/logs follow RFC 7807 error format and structured Pino logging with propagated request ID.

For a new Rust Billing Service, follow platform patterns: run on ECS Fargate in us-east-1, minimum 2 tasks spread across AZs, integrate through API Gateway/ALB path and App Mesh mTLS, use Auth RS256 JWT validation via JWKS, store secrets in Secrets Manager with rotation, use blue-green zero-downtime deployments, and avoid Fargate Spot if it is always-on/critical.

At 1500 req/min from one IP, API Gateway limits are not hit (25 req/s is below 2000 steady), but ALB per-IP rate limit (1000 req/min) throttles excess traffic. Roughly 1000 req/min can pass toward Auth containers and about 500 req/min are blocked at ALB.

JWT signing key dependencies include: Auth Service signer (private key), Auth JWKS endpoint (public key publication), API Gateway custom authorizer Lambda, Portfolio Service JWT validation, Notification Service JWT validation, and any additional service that validates Meridian JWTs.

Pipeline is: commit/PR -> code review by Claude Code required before merge -> GitHub Actions runs lint, type-check, tests, image build, push to ECR, deploy to ECS -> production rollout uses blue-green deployment with ALB target group switching for zero downtime.
